statement.markdown:
Given the root node of a binary search tree (BST) and a value to search for, find the node in the BST that the value is equal to.

The BST is defined as follows: the left subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys less than the node's key; the right subtree of a node contains only nodes with keys greater than the node's key; both the left and right subtrees must also be binary search trees.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains a single integer representing the number of nodes in the BST.
The next lines contain a series of node values representing the nodes in the BST, one value per line, in a pre-order traversal (root, left, right).
The last line contains a single integer representing the value to search for.
statement.outputFormat: The value of the node in the BST that matches the search value, or -1 if no such node exists.

constraints.variable: Number of nodes in the BST
constraints.description: The number of nodes in the BST.
The values of the nodes in the BST.
The value to search for in the BST.

examples.explanation: The BST is constructed as follows:       4      / \     2   6    / \   / \   1  3 5  7The search value 6 is found in the BST.
The BST is constructed as follows:       4      / \     2   6    / \   / \   1  3 5  7The search value 2 is found in the BST.
The BST is constructed as follows:       4      / \     2   6    / \   / \   1  3 5  7The search value 8 is not found in the BST.

hints.text: The problem involves finding a specific node in a binary search tree. Recall that a BST has a particular structure that allows for efficient searching.

In a binary search tree, for any given node, all elements in its left subtree are less than the node, and all elements in its right subtree are greater than the node. This property can be used to guide the search.

The search can be performed by starting at the root and at each step moving either left or right depending on whether the target value is less than or greater than the current node's value. This process continues until the target value is found or it is determined that the value is not in the tree.

editorial.title: Efficient Search in a Binary Search Tree
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved efficiently by leveraging the properties of a Binary Search Tree (BST), where for any given node, all elements in its left subtree are less than the node, and all elements in its right subtree are greater than the node.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve traversing the entire tree in the worst case, resulting in a time complexity of O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the BST. This approach is inefficient for large trees.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach takes advantage of the BST property to search for the target value in O(h) time complexity, where h is the height of the tree.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution starts at the root and compares the target value with the current node's value. If they are equal, the search is successful. If the target is less than the current node's value, the search continues in the left subtree; otherwise, it continues in the right subtree. This process repeats until the target is found or a leaf node is reached.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that the BST property ensures that for any node, all values in its left subtree are less than the node's value, and all values in its right subtree are greater. Thus, if the target value is in the tree, it must be in the appropriate subtree based on its value relative to the current node. If the target is not found after traversing down to a leaf node, it is not in the tree.
editorial.dryRun: For example, given a BST with nodes (8, (3, (1, None, None), (6, (4, None, None), (7, None, None))), (10, None, (14, (13, None, None), None))) and a target value of 6, the optimal solution will find it in O(h) time, where h is the height of the tree.
commonMistakes: Not recognizing the BST property and attempting a brute force or linear search approach.
Failing to handle the case where the target value is not in the tree.
Incorrectly traversing the tree, such as visiting nodes in the wrong order or missing nodes.

functionSignature.java: public int searchBST(TreeNode root, int val);
functionSignature.python: def searchBST(self, root: TreeNode, val: int) -> int:
